content.text: The deployment topology of a Claude integration determines who can call the model, how rate limits are shared across teams, where application logic lives, and how the system behaves under load. A topology that works for a prototype can create rate-limit contention, auditability gaps, or latency problems when deployed at production scale.

Blueprint topology templates codify the patterns the Design Review Board has approved for each class of integration. Delivery teams select the template that matches their requirements and fill in the deployment-specific values — they do not design the topology from scratch.

The topology decision is one of the hardest to change after go-live. The system prompt can be tuned; the retrieval strategy can be swapped; but changing the topology (from direct to proxy, or from synchronous to async) typically requires re-architecting the integration. Get it right at blueprint selection time.

attrs.alt: Four Claude app deployment topologies: direct API, proxy gateway, backend service, and async worker

content.text: The client application calls the Anthropic API directly, using an API key embedded in the application configuration. There is no intermediate service layer.
Prototypes and proof-of-concept integrations not handling production data
Internal developer tools where a single developer or small team is the user base
Batch scripts run by a single user under their own API key
Integrations where the Anthropic API is the only external dependency
Any integration accessed by more than one team — no way to share rate limits equitably
Any integration handling confidential data — API key exposure in the client creates an unacceptable credential risk
Any integration requiring audit logging of all Claude calls — the client cannot guarantee this without a server-side component
Any integration that will move to production — the topology must change before production readiness gates can be satisfied

# direct_api_example.py  (prototype only — not for production)
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="sk-ant-...")  # never hardcode in production

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise this document: {text}"}]
)
print(response.content[0].text)
All API calls from client applications route through a corporate-managed proxy or API gateway. The proxy holds the Anthropic API key, enforces authentication, and pools rate limits across all teams.
Authenticate to the proxy with corporate credentials (OAuth2, mTLS, or API token). They never hold the Anthropic API key.
Validates the caller's identity, applies per-team rate limits, injects the Anthropic API key, forwards the request to the Anthropic API, and logs the call.
The proxy shares the organisation's aggregate API rate limit across teams, with per-team quotas enforced at the proxy layer.
Every request is logged with caller identity, timestamp, model, token counts, and response latency before the response is returned to the client.

# proxy_client.py — client-side code for proxy topology
import httpx
import os

PROXY_BASE = os.environ["CLAUDE_PROXY_URL"]  # e.g. https://claude-proxy.internal/v1

def call_claude_via_proxy(messages: list, max_tokens: int = 1024) -> str:
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['TEAM_API_TOKEN']}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "X-Team-ID": os.environ["TEAM_ID"],
    }
    payload = {
        "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
        "max_tokens": max_tokens,
        "messages": messages,
    }
    response = httpx.post(
        f"{PROXY_BASE}/messages",
        json=payload,
        headers=headers,
        timeout=30.0,
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()["content"][0]["text"]

# proxy_server.py — minimal proxy implementation (FastAPI)
import os
import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException, Depends
from fastapi.security import HTTPBearer

app = FastAPI()
security = HTTPBearer()

ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"]
ANTHROPIC_API_URL = "https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages"

async def verify_team_token(token: str = Depends(security)) -> dict:
    # Validate against corporate identity store
    team = lookup_team_by_token(token.credentials)
    if not team:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid team token")
    return team

@app.post("/v1/messages")
async def proxy_messages(request: Request, team: dict = Depends(verify_team_token)):
    body = await request.json()
    enforce_rate_limit(team_id=team["id"], tokens=estimate_tokens(body))
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.post(
            ANTHROPIC_API_URL,
            json=body,
            headers={
                "x-api-key": ANTHROPIC_API_KEY,
                "anthropic-version": "2023-06-01",
            },
            timeout=60.0,
        )
    audit_log(team_id=team["id"], request=body, response=resp.json())
    return resp.json()
Multiple teams sharing an API rate limit allocation
Organisations that require centralised audit logging of all Claude calls
Client applications that cannot safely hold long-lived secrets
Integrations where the client app is frontend-only (browser or mobile) — the proxy avoids exposing the API key in client code

A dedicated backend service owns the Claude integration logic: it assembles the context (retrieval, tool calls, history), calls the Anthropic API, post-processes the response, and returns a structured result to the client. This is the standard topology for production RAG and agent integrations.
Calls the backend service via an internal API. The client knows nothing about Claude — it receives a structured response.
Owns all integration logic: context assembly, retrieval, tool dispatch, prompt rendering, response filtering, and audit logging.
The backend service talks to the Anthropic API (directly or via proxy), the vector database, and any tool backends.
The client app and the Claude integration can evolve independently. Model changes, prompt changes, and retrieval changes are backend concerns.

# backend_service/main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from services.retrieval import retrieve_context
from services.prompt import render_system_prompt
from services.claude import call_claude
from services.filter import filter_response

app = FastAPI()

class ChatRequest(BaseModel):
    user_message: str
    conversation_id: str
    deployment_id: str

class ChatResponse(BaseModel):
    response: str
    retrieved_doc_ids: list[str]
    latency_ms: int

@app.post("/chat", response_model=ChatResponse)
async def chat(req: ChatRequest) -> ChatResponse:
    import time
    start = time.monotonic()
    context, doc_ids = await retrieve_context(req.user_message, req.deployment_id)
    system_prompt = render_system_prompt(req.deployment_id, context)
    raw_response = await call_claude(
        system_prompt=system_prompt,
        user_message=req.user_message,
        conversation_id=req.conversation_id,
    )
    filtered = filter_response(raw_response)
    latency_ms = int((time.monotonic() - start) * 1000)
    return ChatResponse(
        response=filtered,
        retrieved_doc_ids=doc_ids,
        latency_ms=latency_ms,
    )
Any RAG integration — retrieval logic belongs in the backend, not the client
Any agent integration — tool dispatch and multi-turn state management require a server-side component
Any integration with response filtering or PII redaction requirements
Integrations where the client is a frontend application and must not hold secrets

Work items are placed on a message queue. One or more worker processes consume the queue, call Claude, and write results to a backing store or emit a callback event. No user is waiting synchronously — the user polls for the result or receives a webhook callback.
Any service that creates work items: a REST API endpoint, a scheduled job, a file upload trigger, or an event bus subscription.
A durable message queue (SQS, RabbitMQ, Google Pub/Sub) that holds work items until a worker picks them up. The queue provides durability and back-pressure.
One or more stateless worker processes that consume items from the queue, call Claude, and write results. Workers can be scaled independently of the producer.
Workers write results to a database or object store keyed by the work item ID. Callers poll for results or receive a webhook notification on completion.

# worker/claude_worker.py
import json
import boto3
from services.claude import call_claude
from services.result_store import write_result

sqs = boto3.client("sqs")
QUEUE_URL = "https://sqs.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com/123456789/claude-work-queue"

def process_one(message: dict) -> None:
    body = json.loads(message["Body"])
    item_id = body["item_id"]
    prompt = body["prompt"]
    model = body.get("model", "claude-sonnet-4-6")

    result = call_claude(model=model, user_message=prompt, max_tokens=2048)
    write_result(item_id=item_id, result=result, status="complete")
    sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL, ReceiptHandle=message["ReceiptHandle"])

def run_loop():
    while True:
        messages = sqs.receive_message(
            QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
            MaxNumberOfMessages=10,
            WaitTimeSeconds=20,  # long polling
        ).get("Messages", [])
        for message in messages:
            try:
                process_one(message)
            except Exception as exc:
                log_failure(message, exc)
                # Do NOT delete — let SQS return to queue for retry
Document processing pipelines (contract review, report generation) where users accept asynchronous delivery
High-throughput batch jobs (overnight processing, bulk data enrichment) where the request rate exceeds synchronous API concurrency limits
Integrations where peak request volume is bursty — the queue absorbs spikes and workers process at a steady rate
Any integration where the Claude call may take more than 30 seconds, which exceeds the timeout of most synchronous HTTP clients
Use this matrix to select the appropriate template. When more than one topology applies, choose the one with the lower complexity that still meets the requirements.

All topologies except the async worker operate synchronously and compete for the same API rate limit bucket. The proxy and backend service topologies enforce per-team quotas at the application layer; the async worker topology absorbs bursts in the queue rather than dropping requests.

# rate_limiter.py — token bucket for per-team rate limiting at the proxy
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from threading import Lock

@dataclass
class TokenBucket:
    capacity: int        # max tokens in bucket
    refill_rate: float   # tokens per second
    tokens: float = field(init=False)
    last_refill: float = field(init=False)
    lock: Lock = field(default_factory=Lock, init=False)

    def __post_init__(self):
        self.tokens = float(self.capacity)
        self.last_refill = time.monotonic()

    def consume(self, amount: int) -> bool:
        with self.lock:
            now = time.monotonic()
            elapsed = now - self.last_refill
            self.tokens = min(self.capacity, self.tokens + elapsed * self.refill_rate)
            self.last_refill = now
            if self.tokens >= amount:
                self.tokens -= amount
                return True
            return False  # rate limited
All topologies must handle transient Anthropic API errors: rate limit responses (429), server errors (5xx), and network timeouts. The standard retry policy for production integrations:
HTTP 400 (bad request — fix the payload), HTTP 401/403 (authentication — fix the key).
Exponential backoff starting at 1 second, doubling each retry, capped at 60 seconds, with ±20% jitter to prevent thundering herd.
4 attempts for synchronous topologies (direct, proxy, backend); unlimited for async workers (the queue handles back-pressure).
After 5 consecutive failures, open the circuit for 30 seconds. Return a cached or degraded response rather than queuing more requests against a failing endpoint.

Four deployment topology templates cover the full range of Claude integration patterns in enterprise environments. The Direct API template is for prototypes only. The Proxy Gateway template centralises rate limit management and audit logging for multi-team deployments. The Backend Service template is the standard choice for production RAG and agent integrations, where retrieval, tool calling, and response filtering belong in a dedicated service layer. The Async Worker template handles high-throughput batch workloads and long-running generation tasks where synchronous response is not required.

Topology selection is a one-time architectural decision recorded in the blueprint. Teams that select the right topology at blueprint adoption time avoid expensive refactoring when the integration scales to production volumes.
